When One Database Isn’t Enough
Imagine you’re writing a book.
You finish the first draft after months of hard work.
Now imagine keeping only one copy of it on your laptop.
One accidental deletion.
One hard disk failure.
Months of work disappear forever.
Would you take that risk?
Probably not.
Most of us immediately create multiple copies.
One in cloud storage.
One on an external drive.
Maybe another shared with someone we trust.
Software systems think exactly the same way.
Keeping only one copy of critical data is dangerous.
That’s why production systems use Replication.
Instead of storing data in a single database, they maintain multiple copies across different machines or even different data centers.
If one database server crashes…
Another replica immediately continues serving requests.
For users, everything appears normal.
For engineers, replication is one of the strongest foundations of reliability.

What Happens When the Data Becomes Too Big?
Now imagine your grocery shop has become one of the largest supermarkets in the country.
Every product is stored in one gigantic warehouse.
Eventually workers spend more time walking through the warehouse than actually serving customers.
Finding products becomes slower.
Managing inventory becomes difficult.
So what do supermarkets do?
They divide inventory.
Groceries in one warehouse.
Electronics in another.
Furniture somewhere else.
The work becomes manageable.
Databases face the same challenge.
When one database becomes too large, engineers often split data across multiple servers.
This technique is called Data Partitioning (or Sharding).
Instead of asking one database to do everything…
The workload is distributed.
This improves performance.
It also improves reliability because one overloaded database no longer affects the entire system.
Of course, partitioning introduces new challenges.
Finding data becomes more complicated.
Queries across multiple partitions become slower.
Backups become harder to coordinate.
Like every architectural decision, reliability comes with trade-offs.

Sometimes the Fastest Database Query Is No Query at All
Imagine a café where every customer keeps asking,
“What’s today’s special?”
Instead of running to the kitchen every single time…
The café writes today’s menu on a large board near the entrance.
Customers simply read it.
The kitchen becomes less busy.
Software systems use the same idea.
Frequently accessed information is stored in a Cache.
Instead of repeatedly querying the database…
Applications retrieve data from fast in-memory storage like Redis or Memcached.
Caching improves performance.
But something else happens.
The database now receives far fewer requests.
Less pressure.
Lower chance of overload.
Greater reliability.
Sometimes reliability isn’t achieved by making systems stronger.
It’s achieved by making them work less.

Reliability Becomes Much Harder in Distributed Systems
Earlier in this series, our application lived on one server.
Life was simple.
Now imagine the same application running across:

• Mumbai

• Singapore

• London

• Frankfurt

• Virginia
Each region contains:

• Application servers

• Databases

• Caches

• Message queues
Thousands of machines communicate every second.
Everything looks amazing.
Until…
A network cable fails.
One region loses connectivity.
Suddenly, different parts of your application see different versions of reality.
Welcome to the world of Distributed Systems.
Distributed systems unlock incredible scalability and availability.
But they also introduce one of the most fascinating trade-offs in computer science.

The Rule That Every Architect Eventually Encounters
Imagine you own two supermarkets.
One is in Ahmedabad.
Another is in Mumbai.
A customer buys the last bag of rice in Ahmedabad.
At exactly the same moment…
Another customer buys what appears to be the last bag in Mumbai.
The two stores haven’t synchronized yet.
Now both believe they sold the final product.
Who is correct?
This simple situation leads us to one of the most famous ideas in distributed computing:
The CAP Theorem
It states that during a network partition, a distributed system can guarantee only two of these three properties:

• Consistency © — Every user sees the same data.

• Availability (A) — Every request receives a response.

• Partition Tolerance (P) — The system continues operating despite network failures.
When the network itself is broken, engineers must choose.
Do we prioritize showing perfectly consistent data?
Or do we prioritize keeping the application available?
There is no magical solution.
Only thoughtful trade-offs.
That is why companies choose different strategies depending on the business.
A banking system usually values consistency more.
A social media feed often values availability.
The correct answer depends on what the customer expects.

Why “Eventually” Is Sometimes Good Enough
Suppose you like a friend’s photo on Instagram.
Your friend refreshes the app immediately.
The like doesn’t appear for two seconds.
Would you panic?
Probably not.
Now imagine transferring ₹50,000 through your banking app.
Two seconds later…
Your balance still hasn’t updated.
Now you would definitely worry.
This is the difference between Strong Consistency and Eventual Consistency.
Some applications require immediate agreement.
Others simply need the data to become consistent after a short period.
Companies like Amazon, WhatsApp, Instagram, and many large-scale platforms often use Eventual Consistency where it makes sense.
Users receive a faster experience.
The system remains highly available.
Eventually, every copy of the data reaches the same state.
The word “eventually” doesn’t mean unreliable.
It means the system intentionally accepts temporary differences to gain better availability and scalability.

The Company That Started Breaking Its Own Systems
Most companies try to prevent failures.
Netflix had a different idea.
“What if we intentionally create failures before they happen in production?”
That question changed modern reliability engineering forever.
Netflix engineers built a tool called Chaos Monkey.
Imagine logging into your cloud dashboard one morning and discovering that random production servers are being shut down…
On purpose.
Sounds terrifying.
But that was exactly the goal.
If randomly shutting down one server causes the entire platform to collapse…
The architecture isn’t reliable enough.
Instead of waiting for hardware failures…
Netflix simulates them continuously.
The engineering team observes:

• Does traffic automatically move elsewhere?

• Do new servers start automatically?

• Do customers notice anything?

• Does monitoring detect the problem?

• Can the platform recover without human intervention?
If the answer is yes…
The system passes.
If not…
The architecture improves.
This philosophy became known as Chaos Engineering.
Today many organizations perform controlled failure testing because the safest time to discover weaknesses is before real customers do.

A Reliable Production Architecture
A modern reliable application often looks something like this.
Users
│
▼
DNS
│
▼
Global Load Balancer
│
┌───────────────┴───────────────┐
▼ ▼
Region A Region B
│ │
App Servers App Servers
│ │
Redis Cache Redis Cache
│ │
└───────────────┬───────────────┘
▼
Primary Database + Replicas
│
▼
Backup & Object Storage
│
▼
Monitoring • Alerting • LoggingNotice something interesting.
There isn’t one magic component responsible for reliability.
Reliability is created by layers.
Each layer protects another.
If one fails…
Another continues.
Like the backup generator in our hospital story.

Reliability Has a Price
Many beginners believe every application should aim for 99.999% uptime.
In reality…
Higher reliability always costs more.
More servers.
More databases.
More monitoring.
More engineers.
More operational complexity.
That’s why architects constantly balance business needs with engineering costs.
There is no perfect architecture.
Only the architecture that best fits your business.

What Interviewers Really Want to Know
When interviewers ask about reliability, they’re rarely looking for dictionary definitions.
They’re trying to understand how you think.
Some common questions include:

• What is the difference between Reliability and Availability?

• Explain SLA, SLO, and SLI with an example.

• How would you remove a single point of failure?

• What happened during Meta’s 2021 outage, and what lessons did the industry learn?

• What is replication, and why is it important?

• Explain the CAP Theorem in simple terms.

• Where would you choose eventual consistency over strong consistency?

• What is Chaos Engineering, and why did Netflix create it?
A senior engineer doesn’t immediately suggest adding more servers.
They first ask:

• What is the business impact if this component fails?

• How quickly can we recover?

• How can we detect failure before customers report it?
That’s architectural thinking.

Best Practices: Building Reliable Systems from Day One
Whether you’re building a side project or the next global platform, these principles will serve you well:

• Eliminate single points of failure.

• Design for recovery, not perfection.

• Automate health checks and failover.

• Monitor everything that matters.

• Back up critical data regularly.

• Test disaster recovery — not just backups.

• Keep deployments small and reversible.

• Introduce redundancy where the business truly needs it.

• Regularly test your system under failure conditions.

• Remember that customer trust is easier to lose than to rebuild.
Reliability isn’t a feature you add later.
It’s a mindset that influences every architectural decision.

What Changed My Thinking Today?
Before today, I thought reliability simply meant building software with fewer bugs.
Now I see it differently.
Reliable systems aren’t the ones that never fail.
They’re the ones that continue serving users when servers crash, networks fail, deployments go wrong, or entire regions become unavailable.
The most important lesson wasn’t technical.
It was philosophical.
Failure is inevitable.
Ignoring it is a choice.
Preparing for it is engineering.

Up Next: Day 6
Today we learned how systems earn users’ trust by surviving failure.
But another question naturally follows.
How do companies keep their systems online almost all the time?
In the next article, we’ll explore Availability — the concept behind “Five Nines” uptime, planned maintenance, disaster recovery, active-active vs. active-passive architectures, and how companies like Google, AWS, and Cloudflare deliver services that users expect to be available every hour of every day.
Because in distributed systems…
Being reliable is important.
Being available is what your users experience.
